case_name: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
facts: His Holiness Kesavananda Bharati Sripadagalvaru, the head of the Edneer Mutt, challenged the Kerala Land Reforms Act which sought to divert the property of his religious mutt. The core question was whether Parliament's power to amend the Constitution under Article 368 was unlimited, or whether there were inherent limitations.
judgment: By a 7-6 majority, the Supreme Court established the Basic Structure Doctrine — Parliament can amend any provision of the Constitution, but cannot alter its basic structure. Basic structure includes: supremacy of the Constitution, republican and democratic form of government, secular character, separation of powers, federal character, sovereignty and integrity of India, and judicial review.

case_name: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
facts: Maneka Gandhi's passport was impounded by the government under the Passport Act 1967 without giving her a hearing or reason. She challenged this action as violating her fundamental rights under Articles 14, 19, and 21 of the Constitution.
judgment: The Supreme Court held that the procedure established by law under Article 21 must be right, just, and fair — not arbitrary, fanciful, or oppressive. Articles 14, 19, and 21 are not mutually exclusive but form an interconnected trinity. Any law depriving personal liberty must satisfy the requirements of all three articles. The right to travel abroad is part of personal liberty under Article 21.

case_name: Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India
facts: A challenge to the Aadhaar scheme raised the question of whether the right to privacy is a fundamental right under the Indian Constitution. The government argued that privacy was not a fundamental right, relying on earlier decisions in M.P. Sharma and Kharak Singh cases.
judgment: The Supreme Court unanimously held that the right to privacy is a fundamental right protected under Article 21 of the Constitution. Privacy includes bodily autonomy, informational privacy, and privacy of choice. Overruled contrary observations in M.P. Sharma and Kharak Singh. Any invasion of privacy must satisfy a three-part test: legality, legitimate aim, and proportionality.
legal_principles: Right to Privacy as Fundamental Right

case_name: Visakha v. State of Rajasthan
facts: Bhanwari Devi, a social worker in Rajasthan, was gang-raped by upper-caste men for trying to prevent child marriage. Women's rights groups filed a PIL seeking enforcement of fundamental rights of working women. The question was whether sexual harassment at workplace violates constitutional rights.
judgment: The Supreme Court laid down the Visakha Guidelines for prevention of sexual harassment at the workplace. These guidelines had the force of law until legislation was enacted. Defined sexual harassment, placed obligations on employers to establish complaints committees, mandated awareness programs. Eventually led to the Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition and Redressal) Act, 2013.

case_name: Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India
facts: Five petitioners challenged the constitutional validity of Section 377 IPC which criminalized consensual homosexual acts between adults. They argued it violated their fundamental rights to dignity, autonomy, privacy, and equality.
judgment: The Supreme Court struck down Section 377 IPC insofar as it criminalized consensual sexual acts between adults. The court held that sexual orientation is a natural and innate attribute, and criminalizing it violates Articles 14, 15, 19, and 21. The right to sexuality and choice of partner is constitutionally protected under privacy and dignity.

case_name: Shreya Singhal v. Union of India
facts: Two women were arrested in Mumbai for a Facebook post criticizing a political shutdown. Section 66A of the IT Act 2000 was challenged as unconstitutional for being vague and creating a chilling effect on free speech.
judgment: The Supreme Court struck down Section 66A of the Information Technology Act 2000 as unconstitutional, holding it violated Article 19(1)(a) freedom of speech and expression. The section was vague, overbroad, and had a chilling effect on free speech. Distinguished between discussion, advocacy, and incitement.

case_name: D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal
facts: A letter petition by D.K. Basu, Executive Chairman of Legal Aid Services, West Bengal, highlighting instances of custodial violence and death. The court took cognizance of the widespread prevalence of torture and deaths in police custody.
judgment: The Supreme Court laid down 11 mandatory guidelines to prevent custodial violence and torture. These include: rights of arrested persons, memo of arrest, right to have relative informed, medical examination every 48 hours, diary at interrogation centre, arrested person may meet lawyer during interrogation, and display all details at police station. Any violation is punishable as contempt of court.

case_name: Mohd. Ahmed Khan v. Shah Bano Begum
facts: Shah Bano, a 62-year-old Muslim woman, was divorced by her husband through triple talaq after 43 years of marriage. She filed for maintenance under Section 125 CrPC. The husband argued that Muslim personal law only required maintenance during iddat period (3 months).
judgment: The Supreme Court upheld Shah Bano's right to maintenance under Section 125 CrPC, ruling that this secular law applies to all citizens regardless of religion. The court held that a divorced Muslim woman is entitled to maintenance from her former husband beyond the iddat period if she is unable to maintain herself. This landmark judgment triggered political controversy and eventually the enactment of Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Divorce) Act, 1986.
legal_principles: Maintenance Rights of Divorced Muslim Women
Section 125 CrPC applies to all religions

case_name: Arnesh Kumar v. State of Bihar
facts: The case arose from misuse of Section 498-A IPC (cruelty by husband/relatives) where arrests were being made routinely and mechanically without proper investigation. The Court examined the power of police to arrest in cases punishable with less than 7 years imprisonment.
judgment: The Supreme Court held that arrest under Section 498-A should not be automatic or routine. Police officers must follow checklist under Section 41(1)(b)(ii) CrPC before arrest. Magistrates must verify compliance before authorizing detention. Non-compliance is punishable as contempt. This applies to all offences punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years.

case_name: Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan
facts: Following the gang-rape of Bhanwari Devi, a social worker in Rajasthan, multiple women's organizations filed PILs highlighting the absence of any law to address sexual harassment at the workplace. The petition asked the court to formulate guidelines to protect working women.
judgment: The Supreme Court established the Visakha Guidelines defining sexual harassment at the workplace and mandating preventive mechanisms. These guidelines operated as law until the Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace Act 2013 was enacted. Created the foundation for workplace safety for women in India.
legal_principles: Judicial Legislation to fill Legislative Vacuum

case_name: M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (Oleum Gas Leak)
facts: Oleum gas leaked from Shriram Industries in Delhi, causing death and injury. The case raised questions of liability of hazardous industries and the Right to Life under Article 21 in the context of environmental protection.
judgment: The Supreme Court established the principle of Absolute Liability for hazardous industries (going beyond the Strict Liability principle of Rylands v Fletcher). Industries engaged in hazardous activities owe an absolute duty to the community. Right to live includes the right to live with dignity and in a pollution-free environment. Compensation must be proportionate to the enterprise's financial capacity.
legal_principles: Right to Clean Environment under Article 21

case_name: Shayara Bano v. Union of India
facts: Shayara Bano challenged the practice of instant triple talaq (talaq-e-biddat) as unconstitutional. She was divorced by her husband through three words spoken at once. The petition argued that this practice violates equality and dignity of Muslim women.
judgment: By 3:2 majority, the Supreme Court struck down the practice of instant triple talaq (talaq-e-biddat) as unconstitutional, manifestly arbitrary, and violative of Article 14. Even assuming it was part of personal law, it did not enjoy constitutional protection as a fundamental right. This led to the Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Marriage) Act, 2019 criminalizing instant triple talaq.

case_name: Indian Young Lawyers Association v. State of Kerala (Sabarimala)
facts: Challenge to the centuries-old practice of prohibiting women aged 10-50 from entering the Sabarimala temple in Kerala. The petition argued this violates the right to equality and freedom of religion.
judgment: By 4:1 majority, the Supreme Court held that the exclusion of women from Sabarimala violates Articles 14, 15, 21, and 25 of the Constitution. Exclusion based on biological/physiological characteristics is a form of untouchability. Right to worship is available to all persons irrespective of gender. Justice Indu Malhotra gave a dissenting opinion on the court's intervention in religious practices. Later referred to a larger bench.

case_name: S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
facts: Multiple state governments were dismissed under Article 356 (President's Rule) by the central government. The dismissed Chief Ministers challenged the proclamations as unconstitutional misuse of Article 356.
judgment: The Supreme Court held that the power under Article 356 is not absolute and is subject to judicial review. The President's satisfaction must be based on relevant material. Secularism is a basic feature of the Constitution. The court can examine whether the proclamation was for valid reasons. Floor test should be the primary method to determine majority.

case_name: Lalita Kumari v. Govt. of U.P.
facts: The case examined whether registration of FIR under Section 154 CrPC is mandatory when information discloses a cognizable offence, or whether police have discretion to conduct a preliminary inquiry before registration.
judgment: The Supreme Court held that registration of FIR under Section 154(1) CrPC is mandatory when information discloses commission of a cognizable offence. Police have no discretion to refuse FIR. However, in certain categories (matrimonial disputes, commercial offences, medical negligence, corruption), a preliminary inquiry of 7 days may be conducted before FIR registration.
legal_principles: Mandatory FIR Registration for Cognizable Offences

case_name: K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (Aadhaar Judgment)
facts: Constitutional validity of the Aadhaar Act 2016 was challenged on grounds that mandatory biometric collection violated the fundamental right to privacy established in the 2017 privacy judgment.
judgment: By 4:1, the Supreme Court upheld the Aadhaar Act for government subsidiaries and tax filings, but struck down Section 57 (allowing private entities to demand Aadhaar). Banks and mobile companies cannot mandate Aadhaar. CBSE and NEET Aadhaar requirement struck down. The court applied the proportionality test from the privacy judgment.

case_name: Olga Tellis v. Bombay Municipal Corporation
facts: Pavement dwellers and slum dwellers in Bombay challenged their eviction by the BMC without notice or alternative accommodation. The petitioners argued that right to livelihood is part of right to life under Article 21.
judgment: The Supreme Court held that the right to livelihood is an integral component of the right to life under Article 21. Eviction of pavement dwellers without following due process is unconstitutional. While no absolute right to encroach public property, the state must follow fair procedure, give reasonable notice, and consider alternative accommodation before eviction.
legal_principles: Shelter as component of Right to Life

case_name: Bachan Singh v. State of Punjab
facts: The constitutional validity of the death penalty under Section 302 IPC was challenged as violating Articles 14, 19, and 21 of the Constitution.
judgment: The Supreme Court upheld the constitutional validity of the death penalty but laid down the 'rarest of rare' doctrine. Death penalty should only be imposed when the alternative option of life imprisonment is unquestionably foreclosed. The court must evaluate both aggravating and mitigating circumstances. Death sentence is constitutional but should be an exception, not the rule.
legal_principles: Life Imprisonment as Rule, Death as Exception

case_name: Hussainara Khatoon v. Home Secretary State of Bihar
facts: A series of articles in the Indian Express revealed that thousands of undertrial prisoners in Bihar jails had served longer periods in jail than the maximum sentence for the offences they were charged with.
judgment: The Supreme Court held that the right to speedy trial is a fundamental right under Article 21. The right to free legal aid is also a fundamental right. Undertrial prisoners who had served beyond the maximum punishment were ordered to be released. This led to massive prison reforms and established the foundation for legal aid services in India.

case_name: Vijay Kumar Ghai v. State of West Bengal
facts: A commercial dispute arose between a company and its directors regarding non-delivery of goods despite payment. A criminal complaint was filed under Sections 406 and 420 IPC. The case examined whether every breach of contract can be termed criminal breach of trust.
judgment: The Supreme Court quashed the criminal proceedings, holding that for Section 406 IPC, there must be specific entrustment of property. A mere commercial dispute or breach of contract without dishonest intention cannot be criminalized. The ingredients of Section 406 and 420 are distinct — cheating requires deception at the inception, while breach of trust requires dishonest misappropriation after lawful possession.

case_name: Inder Mohan Goswami v. State of Uttaranchal
facts: The case involved a dispute over sale of land where the vendors allegedly cancelled the sale agreement after receiving part payment. Criminal charges were filed under Section 420 IPC. The Court examined the necessity of fraudulent intent at the beginning of the transaction.
judgment: The Supreme Court emphasized that to convict someone under Section 420, it must be proved that the accused had a dishonest intention at the time of making the promise. Mere failure to keep a promise at a later stage is not cheating. The court warned against the growing tendency of converting civil disputes into criminal cases to exert pressure.

case_name: Lalita Kumari v. Govt. of U.P.
facts: A minor girl was kidnapped and despite a complaint, the police did not register an FIR. The case raised the question of whether registration of FIR is mandatory under Section 154 CrPC.
judgment: The Court held that registration of FIR is mandatory if the information discloses a cognizable offence. However, in cases of matrimonial disputes, commercial offences, medical negligence, and corruption, a preliminary inquiry may be conducted to ascertain if the offence is cognizable, but must be completed within 7 days.

case_name: R.K. Vijayasarathy v. Sudha Seetharam
facts: The case involved allegations of cheating in a property transaction. The Court examined the essential ingredients of Section 415 (Cheating) and 420 IPC.
judgment: The Court laid down the requirements for Section 415: (i) deception of any person, (ii) fraudulently or dishonestly inducing that person to deliver property or consent to retention of property. Section 420 is an aggravated form of cheating involving delivery of property. Established that civil and criminal proceedings can coexist if ingredients of both are met.
legal_principles: Coexistence of Civil and Criminal Remedies
